text: BEC (LE), or BEC-HELLOUIN, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Brionne, on the Rille. Pop. 745.
BEC-DE-MORTAGNE (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Goderville. Pop. 1,004.

BECAN, a parish in co. Mayo, on the watershed | Frankenstein. BEC of the basins of the Shannon, the Moy, and Lough Corrib. Area 20,303 acres. Pop. 5,589. The three lakes of Mannin, Cahir, and Island, on the N boundary, have respectively an elevation of 267, 231, and 267 ft.

BECANCOUR, a river of Lower Canada, which rises in the townships of Broughton and Leeds, and after flowing in an E direction for 46 m., turns NW 21 m., and falls into the St. Lawrence. It abounds in falls, rapids, and shoals; and is navigable even for canoes in only a few places. Isle Dorval divides its mouth into two channels.—Also a seigniory lying along the lower part of the B. river; about 7 m. long, by 6 m. in breadth. Pop. 2,752.
BECCARIA, a township in Clearfield co., Pennsylvania. Pop. 309.

BECCLES, a parish and market-town of Suffolk, 109 m. NE of London, and 40 m. NE of Ipswich, lying along the river Waveney. Area 1,919 acres. Pop. of p. 4,086.—The town is well-built, and consists of several streets diverging from an extensive central area forming the market-place. The environs are agreeable; and near the town is a good race-course on which races are held in September. The theatre is a neat building, with an assembly-room attached; the town-hall is also a handsome structure. The gaol, which has been much extended and improved, stands apart from other buildings, in an open airy situation. B. was incorporated in 1584. Its magistracy consists of a mayor, 4 aldermen, and 12 councillors. The revenue of the burgh, in 1841, was £2,676 5s. <math>8\frac{1}{2}</math>d., most of which arose from rents. B. is one of the polling-places for the members for East Suffolk. Its principal trade is in coals, corn, and malt, which is much facilitated by the water communication with Yarmouth. In 1832 the corporation of B. obtained an act of parliament for deepening the Waveney, from the town to the point of junction between the river and the Norwich and Lowestoft navigation. This important undertaking has been accomplished, and B. now enjoys all the advantages of a port trading direct to the various parts of the united kingdom, and even to foreign ports, without the necessity of transhipment. A steam-vessel is constantly employed in towing vessels between this town and Lowestoft, and thence to the sea. B. suffered severely from fire, in November, 1586.
BECCONSALE, or HESKETH-CUM-BECCONSALE, a chapelry in the p. of Croston, Lancashire, 10 m. NE of Ormskirk, intersected by the Douglas river. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. 553.
BECELARE, a commune and town of Belgium, in E Flanders, in the arrond. and 6 m. E of Ypres. Pop. 2,169. There are extensive woollen factories here.
BECELEUF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Coulanges. Pop. 1,015.
BECERRIL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NNW of Madrid, on the Manzanares river.
BECERRIL-DE-CAMPOS, a small town of Spain, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Palencia, on the Canal-de-Campos.
BECERRIL-DEL-CARPIO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. N of Palencia, near the Pisuerga.
BECHEME, a town of Belgium, in Luxemburg, 6 m. NE of Hubay.

BECHEREL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Montfort.—The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 11,073 in 1831.—The town is 18 m. NW of Rennes. Pop. 802. It has some trade in flax and grain.
BECHERT'S KOPF, a mountain of Bavaria, in Pfalz or the Rhine circle, between Durkheim and
BECHHOFEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 12 m. SSW of Anspach, on the 1837. Wiesent. Pop. 610, of whom one-fourth are Jews.
BECHIN, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle of Prague, 10 m. SW of Tabor, on the Luschnitz. Pop. 1,966. There are mineral springs here.

BECHTAV, an insulated mountain in the Russian gov. of Orenburg, rising rapidly 3,000 ft. above the plain, and 4,000 ft. above the level of the Black sea. Its five conical summits have given it its name, which, in the Nogay language, signifies 'the Five mountains.' The rock of which it consists is a white trachyte, with small crystals of glassy feldspar. Its base is covered by strata of calcareous rock; and several hot ferruginous springs issue from this rock. There are also hot sulphureous springs at some versts distance.—Kupffer.
BECHTHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of the Rhine, 2 m. from Osthofen. Pop. 1,591. Iron is mined in the vicinity.
BECHTIRKOI, a town of Turkey, in Bulgaria, E of Silistria.

BECHUANAS, an African nation, divided into various tribes, inhabiting the country to the N of the Orange river. Each tribe has its hereditary chief or king, who commonly resides in the largest town. A tribe generally includes a number of towns or villages, each of which has its subordinate chief, acknowledging the supremacy of the principal one, whose power, though great, is controlled by the public meetings of the minor chiefs. All their youth are circumcised between the age of eight and fourteen; and they are exceedingly tenacious of their national customs. Their character, however, is frank and sociable.—Moffat.
BECK, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle of Gueldres. Pop. 150.
BECKBURY, a parish in Salop, 6 m. NE of Bridgenorth. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 312.
BECKELSHEIM, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the circle of Warburg. Pop. 1,300.
BECKENHAM, a parish and village in Kent, 9 m. SSE of London, on the line of the Croydon railway. Area 3,820 acres. Pop. 1,608.
BECKERMET (St. Bridger's), a parish in Cumberland, 3\frac{1}{2} m. S by E of Egremont. Area 4,640 acres. Pop. 630.
BECKERMET (St. John's), a parish adjoining the above. Area 3,030 acres. Pop. 468.
BECKET, a township in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 118 m. W of Boston, stretching up the Green Mountain range. Pop. 1,342.
BECKETT, a tything in the p. of Shrivenham, Berks, 5 m. SW of Farringdon. Pop. 42.
BECKFORD, a parish and village in Gloucestershire, 6 m. ENE of Tewkesbury. Area 2,650 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Baugrove, 461.
BECKHAM (East and West), two adjoining parishes in Norfolk, the former 4 m., the latter 5 m. W by S of Cromer. Area of East B. 790 acres. Pop. 56. Area of West B. 780 acres. Pop. 179.
BECKHAMPTON, a tything in the p. of Avebury, in Wiltshire, 6 m. W of Marlborough. Pop. 155.

BECKINGHAM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 43 m. E of Newark-upon-Trent. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Sutton, 462.—Also a p. and v. in Nottinghamshire, 3 m. W of Gainsborough, on the Trent. Area 3,010 acres. Pop. 491.
BECKINGTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. NE of Frome. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 1,190, of whom 113 were in the hamlet of Rudge.
BECKLEY, a parish, partly in Buckinghamshire. partly in Oxfordshire, 4 m. NE of Oxford. Area 4,370 acres. Pop. 675.—Also a p. in Sussex, 61 m. WNW of Rye. Area 5,540 acres. Pop. 1,412.
BECKSTEIN, a village of Austria, in Salzburg, in the district of Linz, in the Gastein valley. Gold has been found in the vicinity.
BECKUM, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in BED the circle and 20 m. SE of Munster. Pop. 1,958 in

BECKWITH, a township in the Bathurst district, in Upper Canada. Pop. 552. A number of lakes, merely connected together by small streams, are scattered over the S part of this township; and on the NE is a larger lake called Wolf lake.
BECON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Le Louroux-Beconnais. Pop. 1,543.
BECSE (O), or ALT BECSE, a district and town of Hungary, in the com. of Bacs, on the r. bank of the Theiss. Pop. 11,132. It has a large trade in grain.
BECSE (UJ), or NEU BECSE, or TOROK BECSE, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Torontal, on the l. bank of the Theiss, opposite O Becse. Its chief trade is in fruits.

BECSKEREK (NAGY), or Gross Becskerek, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Torontal, 45 m. SW of Temesvar, on the Bega. Pop. in 1827, 12,623, of whom 8,841 were Slavonians, Greeks, or Raitzes, and 3,142 Catholics. Silk-worms are extensively reared here.
BECTIVE, a parish in co. Meath, 4 m. NE of Trun. Area 3,386 acres. Pop. 602. The ruins of Babbey, on the banks of the Boyne in this p., are interesting.
BECTIVE-BRIDGE, a village in the p. of Balsores, in co. Meath, on the r. bank of the Boyne. Pop. 142.
BECVURITO, or BACVURITO, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, 45 m. NW of Sinaloa.
BECZKO, or Betzko, a village of Hungary, in the circle of Trentshin, on the Waag river.-Also a v. of Poland, in the circle of Kielce. Pop. 450.
BEDAK, a large village of Afghanistan, in the Koh Daman, finely situated on an eminence, and surrounded with gardens, vineyards, and orchards.
BEDAKSHAN. See BADAKSHAN.

BEDALE, a parish and town in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 34 m. NW of York. The p. has an area of 7,070 acres. Pop. 2,803. It comprises the townships of B., Langthorne, Askew, Burrell, Crakehall, and Firby, and the hamlet of Randsgrange.—The town is situated on a small tributary of the Swale, in a finely cultivated district. Pop. in 1801, 1,005; in 1841, 1,250.
BE'DAMANGALAM, a mountain of India, in Mysore, rising on the borders of the Barah Mahall. Alt. 2,435 ft.

BADARIEUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault.—The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 11,333.—The com. and town are on the Orbe, 20 m. N of Beziers. There are extensive woollen manufactures here. Leather, paper, and glass are also manufactured.

BEDARRIDES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse.—The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 9,026.—The com. and town are on the Orivèze, 10 m. NNE of Avignon. Pop. 2,215. BEDAS, BEDDAS, or VEDDAS, a tribe of Ceylonese, occupying the more secluded parts of the island, along the mountains of Kandu and Karre on the E, the high lands of Bintane on the N, and the country behind Trincomalee. They are generally of low stature, thick set and muscular. Their arms are a very long bow and arrows; in the use of which they are very expert. They appear to worship the Vogaha or Ficus religiosa, but are said to offer sacrifices to Buddha also. Their forests are divided among different families, and each keeps strictly within its own boundaries. They have little intercourse with their neighbours, except for the purpose of obtaining salt, coarse clothing, and copper and glass ornaments, for which they exchange betel-nuts, leopards' skins, honey, wax, and timber.
BEDBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhone, 20 m. NE of Aix-la-Chapelle. Pop. 660.
BEDBURN (NORTH and SOUTH), two townships in the p. of St.-Andrew-Auckland, in Durham, the former 6 m., the latter 7 m. NW of Bishop-Auckland. Pop. of North B. 457; of South B. 350.

BEDDGELART, or BETHGELERT, a parish and village in Caernarvonshire, 12 m. SE of Caernarvon. Pop. 1,397. The hamlet of Nantmor, in this p., is in Merionethshire.—The village of B. is much resorted to by tourists, on account of its proximity to Snowdon, and some of the finest mountain-scenery in Wales.
BEDDINGHAM, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. SE of Lewes. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 268.

BEDDINGTON, a parish in Surrey, 12 m. S of London. Area 3,830 acres. Pop. 1,453. The Wandle has its sources in this p. and at Carshalton, in singularly copious springs, the overflow of the great chalk-basin of London.-Also a township in Washington co., in the state of Maine, U.S. Pop. 164.—Also a township in Berkley co., Virginia.
BEDE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 3 m. N of Montfort. Pop. 2,586.
BEDEGUNA, a district and town of Africa, in Bornu, 12 m. NNE of Lake Zumbrum, in N lat. 12° 22'. The territory includes numerous towns and villages, and produces maize, wheat, and cotton.
BEDEKARFEE, a district and a large and populous town of Bornu, 3 days W of Old Birnie. The Arab women of this place are described as remarkably good-looking.
BEDERKESA, a town of Hanover, in the duchy of Bremen, in the prov. of Stade, on the Meden. Pop. 1,056.
BEDFIELD, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. NNW of Franklingham. Area 1,780 acres. Pop. 358.
BEDFONT (East), a parish in Middlesex, 31 m. ENE of Staines. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 982, of whom 245 were in the hamlet of Hatton.

BEDFORD, the county-town of Bedfordshire, 51 m. NNW of London, 16<sup>1</sup>/<sub>4</sub> m. by railway from the Bletchley station on the North Western railway. The town is intersected by the river Ouse, along both banks of which it extends. It is situated in the middle of the rich tract of country called Bedford vale; and consists of a principal street, nearly a mile in length, intersected by several smaller ones. It contains many ancient but well-built houses, and the general aspect of the town is pleasing. An ancient bridge over the Ouse, forming the communication between the N and S parts of the city, was taken down in 1811, and a new one of five arches erected in its stead at an expense of £15,000. The N approach to the bridge is adorned with an elegant crescent, and new buildings are springing up in various quarters. Races are held here in March. A public library, instituted in 1830, contained, in 1836, 3,980 vols. Besides the churches, the principal public building is the townhall, in which the sessions and assizes are held. At the NW entrance to the town stands the county-gaol, a substantial building, constructed on the plan of the benevolent Howard. The county-house of correction is situated within 300 yards of the gaol. The county lunatic asylum is a handsome erection on the road to Ampthill, capable of containing 65 patients; and the countyinfirmary, founded in 1803, stands on the same road. The pop. of the parish in 1801 was 3,948; in 1841, 9,178. Area 2,200 acres. B. was incorporated by Henry II. By the municipal act of 1835, the government of the borough is vested in a mayor, 6 aldermen, and 18 councillors. It has returned two members to parliament since the 23° of Edward I. The number of electors in 1839-40 was 1,175; in 1842-3, 1,136. The variety and magnitude of the charitable endowments and schools of B. is remarkable. The most impor- BED tant of these is the free grammar-school, founded by Sir William Harpur, in 1561. Its revenues have increased to about £14,000 a-year, and are still augmenting. Every inhabitant of B., renting a house of £10 per annum, may have his son classically educated here. The town is divided into five parishes: viz., St. Cuthbert, St. John, St. Mary, St. Paul and St. Peter's Martin, all in the archd. of Bedford, and formerly in the dio. of Lincoln, but now transferred to the dio. of Ely. The church of St. Paul's is a fine building, with a handsome tower surmounted by a lofty octagonal spire. The Independent old Meeting-house church, Mill-lane, was formed in 1650. John Bunyan, the author of 'the Pilgrim's Progress,' was co-pastor of this church from 1671 to 1688. The name Bedford,— from the Saxon Bedanford or Bedicanford, that is, 'the Lodging at the ford,'—is derived from its situation at an ancient ford on the Ouse; as was also the name of Lettuydur, or Lifwidur, which it received from the later Britons. B. was the burial-place of Offa, king of Mercia, whose tomb, and the chapel which contained it, were carried away by an inundation of the Ouse. A castle of considerable strength was erected here soon after the Conquest. The first earl of Bedford, properly so called, was Ingleram de Coucy, who was raised to this dignity in 46° Edward III. The next who enjoyed the honour was of the blood-royal, John Plantagenet, third son to Henry IV., created duke of Bedford in 2° Henry V.
BEDFORD, a township in the p. of Leigh, in Lancashire, 7 m. NE of Newton-in-Mackerfield. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 4,187.

BEDFORD, a county in the S part of the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Area 1,612 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 29,335. It is a mountainous region, watered by branches of the Juniata and Potomac. Its cap., of the same name, is 103 m. SW by W of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,022. It stands in the midst of a mountainvalley, and is celebrated for its mineral springs.- There is a township of the same name in the co., on the Royston branch of the Juniata. Pop. 1,721.— Also a county in the S part of the state of Virginia, on the E side of the Alleghany range, watered by the head-branches of the Roanoke. Area 600 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 20,203. Its cap. is Liberty.—Also a county in the central part of Tennessee, watered by Duck river and its branches. Area 600 sq. m. Pop. in 1840, 20,546, of whom 4,295 were slaves. Its cap. is Shelbyville.—Also a township in Hillsborough co., New Hampshire, 21 m. S by E of Concord. Pop. 1,555. It is rich in minerals.—Also a township in Middlesex co., in the state of Massachusetts, 15 m. NW of Boston. Pop. 929.—Also a township in Westchester co., New York, 125 m. S of Albany. Pop. 2,822.—Also a township in Cuyahoga co., Ohio, 12 m. SE of Cleveland. Pop. 1,244.— Also a township in Calhoun co., Michigan, on the Kalamazoo river. Pop. 220.—Also a township in Trimble co., Kentucky, 41 m. SW by W of Frankfort. Pop. 148.—Also the cap. of Lawrence co., in the state of Iowa.—Also a township in Monroe co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 500.—Also a township in Coshodon co., Ohio, 60 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 1,141.—Also a township in Meigs co., Ohio. Pop. 567.
BEDFORD (CAPE), a bluff detached piece of table-land, on the coast of Australia, in S lat. 15° 10'.-Also a cape on the coast of Labrador, in Davis' straits, in N lat. 67° 2'.
BEDFORD ISLAND, one of a newly discovered group of islands in the Dangerous archipelago, in the Pacific. Its NW point is in S lat. 21° 18' 30", W long. 136° 38'.

BEDFORD-LEVEL, a large tract of fen country, extending to 400,000 acres, situated in the counties of Norfolk, Suffolk, Huntingdon, Northampton, Lincoln, and Cambridge. Peterborough fen in Northamptonshire, nearly the whole of the isle of Ely in Cambridgeshire, the parts of Holland in Lincolnshire, about 50,000 acres in Huntingdonshire, 63,000 acres in Norfolk, and 30,000 in Suffolk, belong to this great level. It appears to have been formerly inhabited, from the foundations of houses and the trunks of trees which are occasionally found in the soil, and the descriptions of earlier historians represent it as one of the finest and most fertile districts in the island; but in the 13th century repeated inundations of the sea laid waste this tract. Various attempts were made to drain it, especially in the reigns of Henry VI. and Charles I., but without success. This was at last effected by William, earl of Bedford, in 1649, from which circumstance it received the name which it now bears. A large portion of this tract of country has been brought into cultivation, and produces grain, flax, and coleseed, in considerable quantity. Sir John Rennie has recently reported that an additional fall of nearly 6 feet may be gained in the low-water marks of the river Ouse; and that by uniting the Ouse, the Nene, the Welland, and the Witham, and carrying them by one improved outfall into the centre of the great wash, not only will the drainage and navigation of the whole district be greatly improved, but there would be gained in the course of a few years 150,000 acres of new and valuable land. In the fens there are numerous decoys, where vast numbers of wild ducks are annually taken during the winter season, and sent to London.

BEDFORDSHIRE, an inland county of England, situated nearly in the centre of the island, between the parallels of 51° 50', and 52° 21' N lat. It is bounded on the NW and N by the co. of Northampton; on the NE by Huntingdonshire; on the E by the co. of Cambridge; on the SE and S by Hertfordshire; and on the W and SW by Buckinghamshire. Its figure is an irregular oval; its greatest length is about 36 m.; its greatest breadth 23 m. Its circuit is about 150 m. The general aspect of the country is undulating and diversified, the hills being numerous but not lofty, and the valleys neither deep nor extensive. The Chiltern hills cross it in a NE direction; and to the E of these is another small range running in the same direction. The vale of Bedford lies between these ranges; it is a strong clay soil, and is intersected by the Ouse, which flows in a very winding course through it.—This county is watered by three principal rivers: the Ouse, the Ivel, and the Lea. The Ouse rises in the co. of Northampton, and, after passing through Buckinghamshire, enters B. near Turvey, 8 m. W of Bedford, but, from its windings, does not reach that town till after a course of above 25 m. At Bedford it begins to be navigable, and proceeds in a NE direction towards St. Neots in Huntingdonshire. This stream is remarkable for the slowness as well as tortuousness of its course; and is subject to sudden overflows after heavy rains. It receives a few small streams from both sides; but its principal tributary is the Ivel, one branch of which Houghton-Regis; flows E to Limbury, and then SE to Luton, and enters Hertfordshire between East and West Hide. The co. of B. contains an area of 463 sq. m., or 296,320 acres, of which 80,000 are stated to be arable, and 168,000 pasture or common, mostly upon a cold clay soil. The vale of B. is considered one of the finest corn-districts in the kingdom. On the S, the country is much less fertile, being crossed by a ridge of chalk-hills, and covered with a thin soil. Towards the SE corner of the county, there is some rich dairy land. The general aspect of the W portion is flat and sandy, but is favourable to the culture of turnips and beans. In the more favourable parts of the county—as in the vicinity of Biggleswade and in the parish of Sandy-large quantities of vegetables and garden-produce are raised for the supply of the metropolis, as well as the local markets. To the N of Bedford the land is poor, and presents large tracts of iron sand. Farms in this county are seldom of large extent; the average size is about 150 acres. The extent of woodland is now much less than formerly.—The mineral products of this county are of comparatively little value. Fuller's earth abounds in the neighbourhood of Woburn, and was anciently known by the name of Woburn earth. Limestone and coarse marble are abundant. There are numerous mineral springs in this county; some saline, others chalybeate; but none have attained any degree of celebrity. The population of the co., in 1801, was 63,393; in 1811, it was 70,213; in 1821, 83,716; and in 1831, 95,383, of whom 56.8 per cent. were engaged in agriculture, and 25.7 in trade. In 1841, it amounted to 107,936, of whom 90,333 were natives of the co. The pop. between 1801 and 1841 appears to have increased 39 per cent. A large proportion of the female population is employed in the plaiting of straw, and in the manufacture of thread-lace; but this latter branch has much declined since the application of machinery to its processes. A considerable number of mats are made, and a pretty extensive trade in corn, timber, and seed, is carried on. The staples are agricultural produce of all kinds. The county is reckoned healthy; the rate of mortality, on an average of ten years, is estimated at 1 in 56.—The Grand Junction canal, and the London and Birmingham railway, skirt the county to the W of Leighton-Buzzard; and the town of B. is connected with the latter railway by a branch line striking off at Bletchley. B. is in the prov. of Canterbury, and was formerly in the dio. of Lincoln, but has been recently transferred to that of Ely. It forms an archd., and is divided into the rural deaneries of Bedford, Clapham, Dunstable, Eaton, Fleet, and Shefford. It is divided into 124 parishes, of which 56 are rectories, 61 vicarages, and 7 are perpetual curacies and donatives. The number of dissenting congregations in this county, in 1828, was 71. In 1835, the co. contained 208 daily schools, with an aggregate of 6,009 scholars; and 198 Sunday, and 36 infant-schools, comprising in all 15,918 children. B. is divided into 9 hundreds, namely; Barford, Biggleswade, Clifton, Flitt, Manshead, Redbornestoke, Stodden, Willey, and Wixamtree. The area, and pop. in 1841, of these divisions is as follows:- Population. Area in Acres. The county-town is Bedford; besides which there are the market-towns of Dunstable, Ampthill, Biggleswade, Harrold, Leighton-Buzzard, Luton, Potton, Toddington, and Woburn. Two members of parliament are returned for the co.; and two for the borough of B. The registered county voters in 1841 were 4,323; in 1846, 4,287. The influential families of Bedfordshire are those of Russell, Osborn, Pym, St. John, Fox, and Whitbread. The county is included in the Norfolk circuit; and the assizes and sessions are held in the shire-hall at Bedford. The annual value of real property returned in 1815, was £343,685; in 1842-3, £517,474. Previous to the Roman invasion, this county was inhabited by the Cassii, or Cattieuchlani. When the Roman dominion was established, it became part of Britannia Superior; in 310, it was added to the district of Flavia Cæsariensis. During the Saxon heptarchy, it belonged to the kingdom of Mercia; and, on the abolition of two of these kingdoms, it was equally divided between Mercia and Essex. When England was united under one monarchy, B. was included within the Denelege, or Danish jurisdiction. It was the scene of many conflicts with the Danes, in the reign of Edward the Elder; and was one of the first counties to declare against Charles I., at the beginning of the Parliamentary war.
BEDHAMPTON, a parish in Hampshire, 1 m. W of Havant. Area 3,260 acres. Pop. 533.

BEDIAH, a district of Arabia, in Oman, intersected by the parallel of 22° 25' N, 20 m. SE of Ibrah. It contains 7 hamlets, each situated within its own little oasis, which possesses a soil so fertile that nearly every grain, fruit, and vegetable common to India, Persia, or Arabia, is produced by it; and almost at a single step the traveller passes from the glare and sand of the Desert to the shade of noble and stately trees, whose umbrageous foliage the fiercest rays of a noontide sun cannot penetrate. The almond, fig, and walnut here attain an enormous size; and the fruit clusters so thickly on the orange and lime that not a tenth part of it, perhaps, is ever gathered. - Wellsted.
BEDIJA, a small river of La Mancha, in Spain.
BEDINA, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Servia, sanjak and NW of Novibazar.
BEDINGFIELD, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. SSE of Eye. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 336.
BEDINGHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. NW by W of Bungay. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 316.
BEDIRA, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Gaza, on the road from Rama or Ramla to Gaza, 6 m. N of Rama.
BEDIS-VELIZ, or Beliz, a fortress in Marocco, on the coast of the Mediterranean, at the mouth of the Gomera, 140 m. N of Fez. There are extensive forests in the vicinity.
BEDIZZANO, a village of Italy, in Modena, 2 m. NE of Carrara. Pop. 734.
BEDIZZOLA, a district and village of Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. of Brescia, near Chiese. Pop. 2,200.

BEDJA, or Baza, a district on the SW coast of the Red sea, lying between the SE frontier of Nubia, and the N frontier of Abyssinia; and stretching from the parallel of 13° to that of 18° 35' N. Its form is that of an irregular triangle. No natural boundaries on the side of Nubia appear in modern maps; but Arabian authors describe this country as separated from Nubia by a chain of mountains rich in gold, silver, and emeralds. On the E or coastside, beginning at the N extremity of the coast-line, we have Ras Mugdah 32 m. SE of Suakim; and 32 m. SE by S of Ras Assease or Asis. These 64 m. of coast present a low sandy shore, and the country is of the same character several miles inland. A deep bay with soundings of from 5 to 12 fath. is formed between Ras Assease and Ras Shakkul, 121 m. to BED Ras Istye is 31 m. SE 1 E from Ras Shakkul; and between it and Ras Funagene, a distance of 41 m., lies Core Nowarat, the finest bay in the Red sea. About 70 m. to the ESE of the latter bay is Mersa Moobaruk. The intervening coast is all low barren sand, backed by high mountains in the distance. The same character of coast prevails to Arkiko, a further distance of 70 m., which may be regarded as the termination of the B. coast on the SE, and commencement of the Danakil or Samhara territory. The third side of the triangle, or the S boundary of the B. is of very irregular outline. It is formed by the N frontier of the Baharnegash; and that of the districts of Tigre, Shire, Waldubba and Walkayt, in Tigre; and of Shelga and Ras-el-Fil in Amhara. A great proportion of the surface of B. is mere sandy desert. The only large streams which appear to cross it are the Takazze in the extreme SW, and the Mareb towards the centre. Its nomade inhabitants are denominated Bedjah by the Arabian historians. They chiefly subsist on the produce of their flocks of camels, cattle and sheep. Bruce says they speak a dialect of the Abyssinian. M. Quatremère endeavours to demonstrate their identity with the Blemmyes of the ancients; while Abdallah contends that they belong to the race of Berbers or Barabras. We believe that the pop. in the N parts of the B. will be found to be mainly Bisharin Arabs; while in the S districts they are numerously intermingled with Shangalla tribes.

BEDKOW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, circle of Rawa. Pop. 500. BEDLES, BETLIS, or BITLIS, a principality and city of Persia, in Kurdistan, 20 m. SE of Van, and a like distance from Diarbekir. The town extends across the greater part of a wide ravine, open to the E, and distinguished in history as the scene of the defeat of the army of Soliman by the Persians in 1534. Southgate says the mountains form three deep valleys, which come down from the N, SE, and W, to a junction where the city stands; and that it extends itself up into each of them. The houses are of red stone, and generally of two stories, with grated windows to the street. Its castle is situated on the top of a high mountain, on the W side of the plain. The town is not enclosed by walls: but each house forms in fact an independent fortress. Many of the streets run in terraces along the sides of the hills. B. contains four caravanserais, three large and twelve small mosques, eight Armenian churches, and one Nestorian. The bazaars are extensive and well filled. The principal manufacture is coarse striped cotton-cloth, and the chief export is tobacco. The pop. of B. and the neighbouring villages is estimated by Kinneir at 26,000 Kurds, Turks, Armenians, and Syrians. The women display nose-rings, an appendage seldom seen in Persia. The environs are well-cultivated, and produce grain of several kinds, cotton, hemp, rice, olives, honey, and truffles. Pears, apples, plums, apricots, grapes, melons, cucumbers, lettuces, and other vegetables grow luxuriantly. The adjacent mountains form the haunts of lions, wolves, and bears. The territory of B. extends 12 hours towards Sert, 12 towards Mush, 4 towards Diarbeker, and 4 towards Van. It is watered by the Betlischai, or Betlis Su, a head-stream of the Tigris, formed by the junction of three little streams, each descending through one of the valleys which meet at the spot on which B. stands.—Kinneir.— Shiel.—Southgate.

BEDLINGTON, a parish and village in the co. of Durham, but locally in Northumberland, 5 m. SE of Morpeth, on the line of the Berwick and Newcastle railway. Area 8,910 acres. Pop. 3,135, of whom a the SE. Off Ras Shakkul lie the Amarat islands. | large proportion are engaged in coal works in this p., which embraces the townships of B., North Blyth, Chambois, Choppington, Netherton, and East and West Sleckburn. The pop. of B. township in 1841 was 2,023.

BEDMINSTER, a parish in Somersetshire, 12 m. S by W of Bristol. Area 4,180 acres. Pop. 17,862, of whom 17,402 were within the boundaries of the parliamentary city of Bristol in 1841.—Also a township in Somerset co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 8 m. NW of Somerville. Pop. 1,589.—Also a township in Buck's co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 1,640.
BEDNALL, a township in the p. of Baswick, Staffordshire, 3 m. NE of Penkridge. Area 970 acres. Pop. 284.

BEDNYA, a river of Hungary, in Croatia, an affluent of the Drave, which it joins on the r. side. BEDNORE, BIDNUR, or BIDDANORE, a ta'alluk or district of Hindostan, in the NW extremity of the territories of Mysore, situated on the E side of the Western Ghauts, at an elevation of from 4,000 to 5,000 ft. above sea-level. From the greater elevation of the country, the season is a month later here than on the coast. Cattle of small size are bred in the district and exported; the other exports consist of pepper, betel-nut, cardamoms, and sandal-wood; the imports are salt, rice, cocoa-nuts, oil, turmeric, and cotton cloths.

BEDNORE, the cap. of the above district, and formerly the cap. of Canara, in N lat. 13° 50', E long. 75° 3', 187 m. NW of Seringapatam, is a place of considerable size. At the time of Sultan Tippoo's death it consisted of 1,500 houses, besides huts; and it is said to have been once a splendid and magnificent city, containing 20,000 houses, fortified by natural and artificial defences. It was originally named Biderhully, or Bamboo village, until the seat of government was removed to it from Ikeri, and it then became a city of great magnitude and importance, under the name of Bidderuru. At the period of its capture by Hyder Ali its circuit was computed at 8 m., and the plunder taken from it has been estimated at 12 millions sterling. Hyder changed its name to Hydernagur. It was taken again and pillaged by a British detachment, under General Matthews, in 1783, but was speedily reduced by Tippoo and his French allies, and entirely destroyed. General Matthews was charged with a breach of faith; and being conducted in fetters to Seringapatam, was thrown into a dungeon, where, it is supposed, he was put to death by command of the merciless conqueror.
BEDONSKIE-BUDY, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovia, in the circle of Leczyca. Pop. 540.

BEDOUIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Mormoiron, 8 m. from Carpentras. Pop. 2,237. Sand for the manufacture of glass is dug here, and there are extensive potteries. BEDOUINS, BEDOWINS, or BEDWINS, a name given to a vast proportion of the nomade inhabitants of the Arabian and African deserts. It is a corruption of Bedoui, or Beddowee, the Arabian term for 'a Native of the Desert.' They are the Arabes Scenitæ, or 'Arabs dwelling in tents' of the ancients; and occupy the districts, and are the lineal descendants, of the Arabs mentioned in Scripture and by the Greek historians. They retain, moreover, the same manners, customs, superstitions, and prejudices. They trace their origin to the twelve tribes of Ishmael, and hence they are often called Ishmaelites by ecclesiastical writers. Placed in the very regions which were traversed by the Israelites under Moses, their usages and habits bear the strongest resemblance to those of the Jewish patriarchs, who were nomades in the strictest sense; and from no source can more copious illustrations of the Mosaic accounts be drawn, than from the manners and customs of the B., even of the present day. They live in encampments, pitching their tents wherever they find pasturage, and changing their abode as often as the support of their cattle, or the approximation of an enemy, makes a change of place expedient. They never occupy towns or even houses, nor engage seriously in agriculture, being strictly a pastoral nomadic people, supporting themselves almost entirely on the produce of their flocks and herds, BED or the hire of their camels. Sometimes they sow a little Indian corn or barley in any fertile spot, and return to reap it at the proper season; but their efforts are too partial and desultory to form a habit. The milk and flesh of their cattle, and the few esculent vegetables found in the Desert, provide them with food; while the fleeces and skins of their sheep and camels furnish the materials for their clothing; the remainder of their wants are supplied by plunder-a resource to which they seldom scruple to apply, unless restrained by fear, or by the rights of hospitality, a virtue which they hold in the greatest esteem. Sometimes they form themselves into hordes of petty merchants, wandering over the country, and trading in such goods as they can most easily transport from place to place. They are early trained to the exercise of arms and horsemanship, which the continual jarrings of so many independent tribes render necessary to the protection and defence of all. The care of the flocks is most frequently abandoned to the women of the tribe, while the youth are ever on horseback, and in the field, practising the use of the bow, the javelin, and the spear. The heat of the climate, and their life of continual exercise and abstinence, render them extremely spare in form and light in limb; but they are well-formed, active, and alert, and usually have long thin expressive countenances, bright black eyes, and teeth of a pearly whiteness. In stature, they are of a middle size, and their complexion is generally of a tawny hue, or a dusky or yellow brown; but Volney says that some of them are black. Their hair is black and wiry, and their beards thin. The tribes who inhabit the middle of the Desert, have locks somewhat crisped, and approaching the woolly hair of the Negro. The two ends of the shawl which forms their turban are allowed to hang down upon the shoulder, and this forms one of the peculiarities by which the B. dress is distinguished from that of other Arabs. The men wear a thick dark brown baracan of wool, five or six yards in length, and about two in width, which serves them as a dress by day, and a bed by night. Their sheikhs wear very wide sleeves to their robes, and occasionally girdles richly embroidered. The Lex Talionis, or law of retaliation, is one of the distinctive marks of the B.; but their love of hospitality is a not less remarkable characteristic. Even an enemy is secure if he can flee for refuge into the tent. Ali Bey (Don Pedro de la Badia) tells us, that when one of the B. heard that his wife had given food to his foe, who had asked for charity at his tent, not knowing whom it was, he replied,—"I should probably have killed my enemy had I found him here; but I should not have spared my wife, had she forgotten the law of hospitality." The wandering life of the B. affords more freedom to their women than is generally the lot of Moslem females; and the Desert is in many cases the actual scene of the keen passions depicted in Arabian poems and tales. The B. lives in continual action; while temperate diet, constant exercise, and the cheering aspect of an unclouded sky, preserve unimpaired the elasticity of his spirits, and leave him all the enjoyments of a lively sensibility. But the volatile sprightliness of animal spirits, whatever show it may have of intellectual superiority, is never able to avail itself of the benefits of discipline. Bodily activity indeed keeps the mind in health, but it binds it closer to the senses; it repairs the thinking organ, but, at the same time, suspends its use. The robust athlete cannot be "sicklied o'er with the pale cast of thought." The current of his blood is too strong,—his respiration too deep drawn and audible, -not to disturb the halcyon reign of the intellectual essence. The child of the Desert, reared in continual wandering, possesses in the fullest degree the activity of sense; his spirit is all abroad in his perceptive organs; he is voluble and sagacious, quick, passionate, and sympathetic—but by no means intellectual. He can pour forth a full tide of feelings in copious language; he can listen unexhausted to the longest narratives; but he is not a contemplative being. Reflection would require an effort as incompatible with the restless fervour of his habits as with the torpor of natural stupidity. Quickness of perception and strength of imagination are mental characteristics of the B., but their ignorance is extreme; and Superstition, the child of Ignorance, is nowhere more powerful than among these wanderers of the Desert. -Raphael, Bedouins ou Arabes du Desert. Paris, 1818, 3 vols. 18mo.—Burckhardt's Notes on the Bedouins and Wahabys. London, 1831, 2 vols. 8vo.
BEDOUS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Accous, at the confluence of the Gave d'Aspe and Aydius. Pop. 1,289.
BEDR, or Bedr-Honein, a village of Arabia, in the Hedjaz, 85 m. SW of Medina, on the caravan route from Damascus to Mecca.
BEDRECHEIN, a village of Egypt, 10 m. S of Girjeh, on the l. bank of the Nile, near the supposed site of the ancient Memphis, destroyed by Cambyses 640 B. C.

BEDRETTO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, in the district of Leventina, on the S flank of St. Gothard, 9 m. SW of Airolo. Pop. 633, Catholics. A pass at an alt. of 2,460 metres leads from the valley of B. into the Valais.
BEDRULE, a parish in Roxburghshire, lying along the Teviot, and intersected by the Rule. Pop. in 1801, 260; in 1841, 256. It contains the villages of B., Newtown, and Rewcastle.
BEDSTONE, a parish in Salop, 6 m. NE of Knighton. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 139.
BEDUM, a town of Holland, in the prov. and 5 m. N of Groningen. Pop. 1,190.
BEDUMAH, a cluster of islands in Lake Tchad, in the kingdom of Bornu, in Sudan.
BEDUS, or BADUS, a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons. Alt. 7,500 ft. The eastern branch of the Rhine rises on the E declivity of this mountain.
BEDWARDINE (St. John), a parish in Salop, 1 m. SW of Worcester, and extending into the city. Area 3,730 acres. Pop. 2,663.—The p. of B. St. Michael is wholly within the city of Worcester. Pop. 476.
BEDWAS, a parish partly in Monmouthshire, partly in Glamorganshire, 10 m. W by N of Newport. Area 4,340 acres. Pop. 800. The hamlet of Lower B. had a pop. of 284 in 1841; that of Upper B., 458.
BEDWELTY, a parish in Monmouthshire, 12 m. NW of Newport. Area 15,440 acres. Pop. 22,413.

BEDWIN (GREAT), a borough and market-town in Wiltshire, 71 m. W by S of London. Area of p. 10,420 acres. Pop. 2,178. It contains the tythings of Crofton and Wolfhall, East and West Grafton, Martin, Wexcombe, and Wilton.—The town of B. is 6 m. SE of Marlborough. It is a place of little trade or importance; but returned two members to parliament until disfranchised by the Reform act.
BEDWIN (LITTLE), a parish in Wiltshire, 4 m. WSW of Hungerford. Area 3,770 acres. Pop. 597.
BEDWORTH, a parish in Warwickshire, 3 m. S of Nuneaton. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 4,253.
BEEAH, a village of Sinde, on the r. bank of the Indus, 25 m. N of Hyderabad.
BEEAWUL, a town of India, in the prov. of Candeish, at the confluence of the Surnuddi and Arki.
BEEBAN. See BEHABAN.

BEEBAN-EL-MOLOOK, or BIBAN-EL-MULUK [i. e. 'the Gate or the Vale of Kings'], a rocky ravine near the banks of the Nile, to the SW of Kornah, in which Belzoni discovered a magnificent sepulchre, supposed to be that of Psammis or Psammethis, the son of Necho, whose war against the Jews and Babylonians is recorded in the 35th chap, of 2d Chron. -Belzoni's Narrative. Lond., 1820.-Quart. Rev., vol. xxiv.
BEEBY, a parish of Leicestershire, 6 m. NE of Leicester, on the line of the Midland Counties railway. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 115.
BEECH-HILL, a tything in the p. of Strathfieldsaye, Berks. Area 770 acres. Pop. 261.

BEEDER, or BIDER, a prov. of Hindostan, in the Nizam's dominions, in the Deccan; bounded on the N by Berar and Aurungabad; on the E by Hyderabad and Gundwana; on the S by Hyderabad; on the W by Bejapur and Aurungabad. It is divided into the seven districts of Calberga, Naldrug, Akulcotta, Calliany, B., Nandere, and Patri. The surface is generally hilly, but nowhere rises into mountains; and the soil is productive. The Manjera and the Godavery are the principal rivers.—Its cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 17° 49', E long. 77° 46', 73 m. NW of Hyderabad. Its walls embrace a circuit of 6 m., and it contains some faint traces of ancient splendour, but the place is of no great strength or importance.—The majority of the pop. of this prov. are Hindus. The Moguls conquered it towards the close of the 17th cent.; and the Nizam of Hyderabad wrested it from the successors of Aurungzebe in 1717.
BEEDING, a parish in Sussex, 1 m. E of Stey- BEE acres. Pop. 1,389, of whom 614 were in the p. of Upper B.; and 775 in the chapelry of Lower B.
BEEDON, a parish in Berkshire, 2 m. S of East Ilsley. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 334.

BEEF ISLAND, one of the smaller Virgin islands, in the W Indies, between Dog island on the W, and Tortola on the E. It is 5 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth.—Also an island at the mouth of the Terminos lagune, in the SE part of the bay of Campeachy.
BEEFELDEN, a town of Hessen-Darmstadt, in the circle of Starkenburg, on the Mumling. Pop. 2,600.
BEEFORD, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. ESE of Great Driffield. Area 5.270 acres. Pop. 977, of whom 766 were in the township of B.
BEEGDEN, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 2 m. W of Ruremonde, on the Meuse. Pop. 531.
BEEJANAGOOR. See BIJANAGUR.
BEEJAPOOR. See BEJAPORE.
BEEK, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 9 m. NE of Maestricht. Pop. 2,358.—Also a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gueldres, S of Deutichem.
BEEKMAN, a township in Dutchen co., in the state of New York, U.S., 14 m. SE of Poughkeepsie, on the Fishkill river. Pop. 1,400.
BEEKMANTOWN, a township in Clinton co., in the state of New York, 8 m. N of Plattsburg. Pop. 2,769.
BEEL, a town of Austria, in Hungary, in the com. of Bihar, on a tributary of the Koros.
BEELERSVILLE, a village in the state of Arkansas, U.S., in Carroll co., near Crooked creek. There are numerous springs about 5 m. to the S of this v.
BEELEY, a chapelry in the p. of Bakewell, Derbyshire, 3 m. E of Bakewell. Area 3,250 acres. Pop. 406.
BEELSBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. E of Caistor. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 181.
BEEMA. See BHIMA.
BEEMASIR, a town in the district of Gogadeva, in Rajasthan.

BEEMSTER POLDER, a fertile district in the prov. of North Holland, in the kingdom of Holland, lying to the NW of Edam, and N of Purmerend, nearly in the centre of the prov. It is in the basin of a drained marsh, extending to about 8,000 acres, and has a pop. of 2,500. Fine cattle are reared in this district.
BEEMTHAL, a beautiful mountain-valley of Northern India, in Kumaon, at an alt. of 3,200 ft. above the sea, and 2,700 above the plain of Rohilcund. It is on the road from Ruderpur to Ramgur.
BEENHAM, a parish in Berkshire, 8 m. WSW of Reading. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 421.
BEENMAHL, or BHINMAL, a town of India, in Rajasthan, midway between Jalor and Sankor. Pop. 6,000.
BEENSHALLIG, a small island of Denmark, in the prov. of Sleswick, S of Gröde.
BEER, a river of Wurtemberg, in the Schwarzwald, an affluent of the Danube.
BEER. See BIR.
BEERALOO, a village of Sinde, near the l. bank of the Indus, 4 m. S of Roree.

BEER-ALSTON, a town in the p. of Beer-Ferris, Devonshire, 6 m. SSW of Tavistock, on the river Tamar. It is a small place, but returned two members to parliament till disfranchised by the Reform act. There are lead-mines in the vicinity.

BEERBERG, a mountain of Germany, in the principality of Gotha-Altenburg, the highest summit ning, intersected by the Adur river. Area 3,500 of the Thuringerwald chain. Alt. 2,985 ft. THE BEREED WINGS, BEE THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF T - 7- Toron Transfer and the contract of - . . - - A . . 1 . 174 1 *
BEER-CROCOMBE, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. NW of Ilminster. Area 770 acres. Pop. 179.

BEEREN (GRoss), a village of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the circle of Teltow-Storkow. Pop. 191. The adjacent v. of Klein B. has a pop. of 154.—A battle was fought in the vicinity of these villages between the French and Prussian armies, on the 24th of August, 1813.
BEERENDRECHT, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, 12 m. NW of Antwerp, on the Scheld. Pop. 1,485.
BEERFELDEN, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Starkenberg, circle of Erbach. Pop. 2,562. Cloth is manufactured here.
BEER-FERRIS, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. NE of Saltash. Area 5,850 acres. Pop. 2,142.
BEER-HACKET, a parish in Dorsetshire, 4 m. SW of Sherborne. Area 450 acres. Pop. 103.
BEERING. See BEHRING.
BEERNEM, a commune of Belgium, in E Flanders, in the arrond. and 5 m. E by S of Bruges. Pop. 2,953.
BEEROTH. See BEIRUT.
BEER-REGIS. See BERE-REGIS.
BEERS, a river of Belgium, in N Brahant, an affluent of the Diest.
BEERST, a town of Belgium, in W Flanders, on the r. bank of the Yperlee, 8 m. SE of Nieuport. Pop. 1,172.
